captions.text: I was wondering what was going on with Hairy Dad  games. It's been such a long time since the last
good old-fashioned AAA action-adventure Hairy  Dad game. Look at the big titles from recent
years. Ghost of Yōtei? Dad only slightly  on the hairy side; dead before the story
even starts. Expedition 33? More of a hairy  polycule situation. And the closest thing
to a hairy dad in Resident Evil Requiem was  Leon Kennedy, who's more of a clean-shaven,
favorite gay uncle. Thank goodness for Capcom's  new one, Pragmata. Which presumably knew it was
going to be an awkward sell. "Hello," it would  say. "I'm a single-player narrative-based game
with no online gameplay or planned DLC, and I'm  based on a completely original IP." Then, talking
louder to be heard over all the shareholders  sprinting for the exits, it adds, "But don't
be afraid that we're taking any risks here because  the plot of the game is basically the archetypal
example of a sci-fi horror premise." And when some  of them were still clearly eyeballing the doors,
it adds, "And would it help if I said that the  main characters so perfectly embody the familiar
Hairy Dad dynamic, you'd think we were shooting  to be the new cover image on the TV Tropes page?"
The story begins with our protagonist  Hugh, presumably short for Huge Man,
your standard hairy dad—burly, white, nondescript  facial hair, more than a passing resemblance to
Burt Reynolds—arriving at a moonbase because he's  a member of whatever authority one notifies when a
moonbase stops responding to calls and needs  a wellness check. The Moon Police, I guess,
or possibly an organization that doesn't  sound like a prog rock band. So of course
the moonbase is mysteriously deserted, and of  course Hugh blunders right in with several of
his fellow soldiers, who all have face-covering  helmets on, and I was like, "Yeah, I don't think
I'll bother committing these characters' names  to memory. I'm just gonna call you Breakfast,
Lunch, and Dinner." So place your bets, people:  is it aliens, rogue AI, or failed objectivist
utopia? And the winner is... rogue AI! Thanks for  playing. The station's mainframe turns hostile;
Hugh gets thrown down a pit and left for dead  while a bunch of evil robots enjoy three delicious
meals. Now, I said this is a bog-standard  horror sci-fi premise, and it is, and that's
weird because Pragmata isn't a horror game at all.  Hugh is rescued by an adorable seven-year-old girl
robot whom he names Diana to memorialize what a  massive car crash this operation has turned into.
And it turns out Diana has the ability to hack  robots and expose their vulnerabilities, so she
and Hugh proceed to have an absolute fucking  blast doing piggy back rides across the base,
shooting up robots in an entirely bloodless  action combat game that's kind of like a Dead
Space Saturday morning cartoon where Isaac Clarke  teams up with a plucky kid sidekick and a goofy
robot mascot. The core combat gimmick is that  you control Hugh and Diana at the same time;
while aiming at an enemy robot, you can  either shoot them with Hugh's big, masculine,
slightly phallic right trigger or you can hack  them by pissing about with Diana's bright,
childish, colorful face buttons. And it works  rather well. Hammering away at the buttons to
move squares around in the hacking game feels the  way hacking always does in movies—lots of random
typing and rapid eye movements—and finishing  it up with a devastating shotgun blast after
the hack pops open the robot's testicle armor  is quite satisfying, and it's paired with some
nice rocket-powered dashes, jumps, and hovers  to give you the traversal power to command the
battlefield. Enemies can tend towards the spongy,  and your guns tend to run out of ammo very fast,
but that just further incentivizes you  to switch back to hacking mode while you
wait for your eternally constipated guns to  gruntingly squeeze another shot or two out.
So I'd say Pragmata succeeds at creating  a fun combat loop with a unique twist,
and there are plenty of boss fights, challenges,  and extra gameplay modes to make the most of it.
It's the story I feel iffier about. It's not bad,  it's just kinda... there. Like a student who gets
straight Bs in every subject and spends every  recess staring vacantly into space while all
the interesting kids kick the shit out of each  other. There's none of the usual conflict one
expects from a Hairy Dad dynamic, where a bond  between characters has to form over time—Hugh
and Diana more or less drop straight into  father-daughter roles. We don't know anything
about Hugh or what history he has with children,  but he's never anything but nice as pie to Diana,
and for her part, Diana kinda oscillates  between "bleep bloop robot no understandy
your human relationships" and being the cutest  little beansprout in the cabbage patch as she
makes crayon drawings of her and Hugh covered  in hearts and "I love my proxy not-dad" slogans
with half the letters adorably drawn backwards  to say thank you for all the collectible toys
and playground equipment you can find throughout  the levels for her. And if you've got any sense of
foreshadowing at all, you'll be inevitably  thinking, "This is not going to end well,
is it?" It's a too-obvious setup of what  we story enthusiasts call "Chekov's Puppy."
A rule that states that the more innocent and  doe-eyed a puppy is, the likelier it is that in
the final act, that puppy will be positioned in  front of Chekov's Bum-Kicking Machine. And when
that inevitably occurs, we do feel sad. But not  because we were really engaged with the character
and their backstory, because it's sad to see a  puppy get kicked. Of course we care for Diana:
she looks like a refugee from a child beauty  pageant and talks like one of the girlfriends
from Alvin and the Chipmunks. Plus, she can blow  up robots with her mind, so she'd be fun to have
around at an Elon Musk press conference. As I  said, the developers clearly felt the need to
play things safe, so the story is a succession of  thin characters slotting into the precise function
required of them by the plot and proceeding to  do the exact thing you would expect. The train
map you see between missions inadvertently  symbolizes the entire experience because
it's on a perfectly circular rail and you can see  exactly where you're going to go. So of course,
the evil AI has been corrupted, and its corruption  manifests late in the game as wibbly-wobbly
purple-and-red badness goo that hurts if you  touch it, and we close things off with a final
boss that's basically a great big amalgamated  blob of wibbly-wobbly purple-and-red badness goo.
And then we get to the ending, and I won't lie,  listeners: I was emotionally affected. As already
established, it's sad to see a puppy being sad.  In other words, it was effective in about the
most uncomplicated way imaginable. The emotional  drama equivalent of a fart joke. And that's why,
for all that I enjoyed the core gameplay loop,  my final word on Pragmata doesn't get any more
enthusiastic than "Well, I didn't dislike it."  Because the story does exactly as much as is
needed to bring the point across and absolutely  nothing more. It clocks off at dead-on 5:00 P.M.
and doesn't even make eye contact as it hurries to  the car park. But, you know, maybe it's enough to
do just enough. Another sentence for the hall of  fame. The worst I could say is that the intended
tone lurches back and forth somewhat: a harrowing  boss fight with a giant, insane robot scorpion
might be immediately followed by us teleporting to  the playground in our home base to push Diana on
the swings for a bit, but that aside, the story  does the job without any nuance or lingering
questions in our minds to ruminate upon. And by  the standards of Capcom games, that's a pretty
good showing. Certainly improves on Resident Evil  Requiem's performance; that only did the job up
until lunchtime and then tripped and knocked  itself out on a water cooler full of old wank.
